did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a two-level meta-ensemble learning technique (stacking) with weighted losses to classify skin lesions (Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, etc.) from dermoscopic images provided by the ISIC 2019 Challenge. It also employs a CS-KSU module for novel class detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a two-level meta-ensemble learning technique (stacking) and a CS-KSU module for skin lesion classification and novel class detection. It details specific methods (weighted losses, triplet loss, ResNet-18), datasets (ISIC 2019 Challenge), and reports new quantitative results (BMA score of 0.591, AUC of 0.544). Although published in CVPR Workshops, it contains original algorithmic contributions and validation results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Learning A Meta-Ensemble Technique For Skin Lesion Classification And Novel Class Detection
journal: 2020 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW)
brief_summary: This study addresses the task of skin lesion classification and novel class detection using a two-level meta-ensemble learning technique trained with weighted losses to mitigate class imbalance. The authors utilized the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset, which contains 25,331 dermoscopic images across eight known classes, to train base classifiers and a stacking model. To handle unknown classes during testing, the algorithm was extended with a CS-KSU module for novelty detection. The primary evaluation approach involved measuring Balanced Multi-class Accuracy (BMA) for known classes and Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) for unknown class detection. The proposed method achieved a BMA score of 0.591 for known classes and an AUC of 0.544 for unknown classes, performing at par with current state-of-the-art methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 8 known classes in the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset used for classification. Mentioned as a curable skin cancer if detected early.
One of the 8 known classes in the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset. Identified as having a significantly higher number of samples (class imbalance).
One of the 8 known classes in the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset.
One of the 8 known classes in the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset.
One of the 8 known classes in the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset. Often referred to as Seborrheic Keratosis in broader contexts, but labeled BKL here.
One of the 8 known classes in the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset. Identified as having far fewer samples compared to NV, MEL, BCC, BKL.
One of the 8 known classes in the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset. Identified as having far fewer samples.
One of the 8 known classes in the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset. Identified as having far fewer samples.
Images belonging to classes not present in the training set (the 8 known classes) are presented to the classifier during testing time. The CS-KSU module is designed to detect these.

all_task_types.target: Known skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on classifying dermoscopic images into one of 8 known diagnostic categories using a two-level meta-ensemble learning technique (stacking). The primary evaluation metric is Balanced Multi-class Accuracy (BMA), which is a classification metric. The model predicts discrete class labels.

The paper includes an extension (CS-KSU module) for detecting images belonging to classes not present in the training set (novel class detection). This is evaluated using the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) for unknown class detection, treating it as a binary classification problem (known vs. unknown).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopic images provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive, accumulated and consolidated from sources including the HAM10000 dataset. It contains images of skin lesions with 8 known diagnostic classes. The dataset is characterized by significant class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training base classifiers (EfficientNet-B2, EfficientNet-B5, DenseNet-161) and the stacking meta-learner via 5-fold cross-validation. 4/5 partitions were used for training base models, and the 5th partition was split for validating base models and training the meta-learner.
use.validation: Used for validating base classifiers and training the meta-learner (via splitting the 5th fold). Also used for validating the CS-KSU modules via 2-fold simulation sets.
use.testing: Used for testing the final model performance. Unknown class images were presented during testing time to evaluate the CS-KSU module's novelty detection capability.
use.other: The dataset was pre-processed using the Shades of Gray algorithm for color constancy. Runtime data augmentation was applied during training.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used at the lower (base) level to train base classifiers and validate them, ensuring out-of-sample instances are used for the meta-learner.
Used at the upper (stack/meta) level to train the meta-learner on out-of-sample instances from the base learners; also used for training and validation of the CS-KSU modules via simulation sets.
Used to split the dataset into K-folds for cross-validation and to create known/simulated unknown class sets for CS-KSU module training, preserving class distribution.
Primary evaluation metric to assess classification performance across imbalanced classes.
Secondary metric used to evaluate unknown class detection capability and to break ties when BMA scores are identical.
Used during training to mitigate class imbalance by assigning different weights to classes based on sample counts.
Used in the CS-KSU modules to learn an embedding space with separability between known and simulated-unknown classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using Balanced Multi-class Accuracy (BMA) and Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) scores, with comparisons made against other teams in the ISIC 2019 Challenge leaderboard without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019 Challenge (Classification Task.evaluation_setting: ISIC Live Leaderboard Test Data (8,238 images)
ISIC 2019 Challenge (Classification Task.notes: The stacking of base learners significantly improved classification performance compared to simple averaging. The proposed method's performance is at par with current state-of-the-art methods. The decrease in BMA for the model with unknown class detection is compensated by an increase in Unknown Class AUC.

ISIC 2019 Challenge (Per-Class AUC Score.evaluation_setting: Comparison of Stacking Module with simple averaging vs. stacked approach vs. stacked approach with unknown class detection
ISIC 2019 Challenge (Per-Class AUC Score.notes: Without unknown class detection, the stacking based approach has better AUC scores for instance-light classes (DF, AK, SCC) compared to simple averaging. For instance-heavy classes (NV, MEL, BCC, BKL), AUC scores remain comparable. The CS-KSU Module Collection improved unknown class detection without significant loss in AUC scores for known classes.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-level meta-ensemble learning technique (stacking) with a Class Specific - Known vs. Simulated Unknown (CS-KSU) module collection for novel class detection.
architecture_used.pretraining: Base learners (EfficientNet-B2, EfficientNet-B5, DenseNet-161) were pre-trained on ImageNet. CS-KSU modules utilized pre-trained ResNet-18 models.
architecture_used.input_size: Base learners accepted random crops resized to specific dimensions: EfficientNet-B2 (320x320), EfficientNet-B5 (456x456 and 300x300 configurations), and DenseNet-161 (224x224). Original images were 512x512.

meta_learner.purpose: Performs out-of-sample generalization over base learner outputs

cs_ksu_modules.purpose: Detects unknown/novel classes by determining if the stacking module's prediction is correct

training_details.base_learner_training: 5-fold cross-validation. 4/5 partitions used for training base models (pre-trained on ImageNet), 5th partition used for validation.
training_details.meta_learner_training: 2-fold cross-validation on out-of-sample instances from the 5th partition of the base learners.
training_details.cs_ksu_training: One-class vs. rest approach. 2-fold simulation sets created for each known class (7 sets per class, total 14 fold-sets per class) using triplet and weighted cross-entropy loss.
loss_functions: Weighted Cross-Entropy Loss (for base learners and CS-KSU)
training_details.preprocessing: Shades of Gray algorithm for color constancy; runtime data augmentation.

classification_or_output_head.stacking_module: Softmax output from base learners aggregated by the 3-layer meta-learner.
classification_or_output_head.cs_ksu_module: Binary classification output (Known vs. Unknown) with empirically selected threshold via grid-search on validation probabilities.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CS-KSU Module Collection (Class Specific - Known vs. Simulated Unknown)
preprocessing_techniques: Shades of Gray algorithm for color constancy (color normalization) to address illumination and acquisition variations

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to mitigate class imbalance and over-fitting. Specific geometric or color transform parameters are not reported in the text, only that it was used in conjunction with weighted loss.

conclusions: The proposed two-level meta-ensemble learning technique (stacking) with weighted losses effectively mitigates over-fitting caused by class imbalance in skin lesion classification.
The stacking approach significantly improves classification performance compared to simple averaging of probabilities, achieving a Balanced Multi-class Accuracy (BMA) score of 0.591.
The performance of the stacking method is at par with current state-of-the-art methods for the ISIC 2019 Challenge task.

Appending the Class Specific - Known vs. Simulated Unknown (CS-KSU) module collection allows for the detection of novel classes during test time, achieving an Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) score of 0.544.

There is a trade-off between classification accuracy and novelty detection: the inclusion of the CS-KSU module results in a decrease in BMA score (to 0.568) which is compensated by the increase in Unknown Class AUC.

Without unknown class detection, the stacking-based approach yields better AUC scores for instance-light classes (Dermatofibroma, Actinic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) compared to simple averaging, while maintaining comparable scores for instance-heavy classes.
The availability of extra data can improve the performance of the proposed architecture.
Future improvements to the CS-KSU Module Collection can be achieved by replacing ResNet-18 components with other state-of-the-art pre-trained models.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., Melanocytic Nevus has far more samples than Dermatofibroma), potentially biasing the model towards populated classes despite mitigation efforts.
Novel class detection performance is limited, achieving an AUC of only 0.544, which indicates near-random discrimination capability for unknown classes.
There is a trade-off in performance: appending the CS-KSU module for novel class detection reduced the Balanced Multi-class Accuracy (BMA) for known classes from 0.591 to 0.568.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; comparisons are based on leaderboard scores without statistical significance testing.
The model was evaluated on the ISIC Live Leaderboard test set (8,238 images) where target labels are not publicly available, limiting independent verification of the reported BMA and AUC scores.

The authors acknowledge that the performance could be improved by using extra data, other pre-trained networks, and further calibration of the CS-KSU module, suggesting current results may not represent the method's full potential.

The novelty detection approach uses simulated unknown classes generated via one-class vs. rest training, which may not fully represent the distribution of real-world novel lesions encountered in clinical deployment.
